Author: Thinking Loop
Title: 7 Times Claude Caught My Logic Before I Asked
Text:
Member-only story
7 Times Claude Caught My Logic Before I Asked
Real moments an AI co-pilot flagged bugs, blind spots, and shaky assumptions — saving hours and a little ego.
Seven concrete examples where Claude pre-emptively debugged my logic — from off-by-ones to caching, SQL joins, and race conditions — plus prompts and snippets you can reuse.
We like to imagine we ask the perfect question and the AI returns the perfect answer. Reality: I ask a messy question, and Claude replies with, “Are you sure about that precondition?” And that’s the whole ballgame. The win isn’t just code generation — it’s the unsolicited guardrails. Here are seven times that “um, actually” saved me from myself.
1) The Off-By-One That Hid in Plain Sight
I was slicing a window of metrics to compute a rolling p95:
def p95(xs):
k = int(len(xs) * 0.95)
xs_sorted = sorted(xs)
return xs_sorted[k] # bug: zero-indexed; 95th percentile should ceil-1
Before I asked for optimization, Claude highlighted: “If len(xs)=20
, k=19
returns the 20th item; typical p95 uses ceil(0.95*n)-1
and bounds checks.” It then proposed:
import math
def p95(xs):
if not xs:
return None
xs_sorted = sorted(xs)…
